item.title: Why don’t we take Father’s Day seriously?
item.description: Even dads don’t care about it — so they say…

item.title: Reaching a nuclear deal with Iran will be much harder than in 2015
item.description: Tehran’s position has hardened and trust has declined in the years since the JCPOA

item.title: A reprieve for gilts
item.description: Plus Asian inflation

item.title: Nigel Farage should be worried
item.description: Fear of a Reform victory led Makerfield voters — including some 2024 Conservatives — to tactically vote Labour

item.title: Are insurers becoming dangerously addicted to private credit ratings?
item.description: We should worry about what happens when regulatory arbitrage goes unchecked

item.title: AI is turning Nintendo and Sony products into accidental luxury goods
item.description: With component-makers busy supplying data centres, console prices are rising as demand outstrips production capacity

item.title: Newby Hall is a glorious lesson in restoring a rock garden
item.description: It seemed beyond repair but this grand mountain scheme in Yorkshire has been revived after decades of decline

item.title: Asics could run faster if it unshackled Onitsuka Tiger
item.description: Sportswear group is separating out its coolest brand but a sale or proper spin-off might be a better fit for shareholders

item.title: South Africa’s anti-migrant backlash
item.description: The country would benefit more if it stuck to its pan-African principles

item.title: The world may not like Trump’s Gaza plan — but there is no alternative
item.description: If even I, an implacable opponent of the president, can accept this as the best option, then surely others can too?

item.title: Robinhood is well placed for the casino-ification of everything
Is Tesla destined to be folded into SpaceX?
item.description: Wall Street simmers with the idea of a merger of Elon Musk’s companies

item.title: Trump is taking a page out of China’s sovereign AI playbook
item.description: Governments have long protected strategic industries — what is new is their willingness to become shareholders

item.title: Is the world becoming more predictable?
item.description: Bigger and better data sets and powerful AI models are allowing us to spot previously undetectable patterns

item.title: Andy Burnham and the search for hope in a hopeless Britain
item.description: The alternative is despair and the uglier politics that heralds

item.title: The unusual experience of watching the World Cup at home
item.description: For the first time in decades, I didn’t see the tournament kick off in the stadium

item.title: Europe lacks a geopolitical strategy for digital money
item.description: It’s a poor goal to make a world of fast technological change safe and cuddly for banks

item.title: China’s tribute system and the new world order
item.description: Exercising power without resorting to war could be Beijing’s ultimate show of strength

item.title: Kevin Warsh’s debut
item.description: Hawkish overtones, dovish undertones

item.title: BMW sounds the alarm as China squeezes Europe’s carmakers
item.description: Chinese manufacturers have grown their market share in Europe while gaining at home

item.title: A football manager gives the G7 a pep talk
item.description: Lessons from one zero-sum competition for another

item.title: What is the point of Berkshire Hathaway?
item.description: It can be bold — or become an index fund

item.title: The risks of borrowing from China’s economic playbook
item.description: Western governments must ensure industrial policy does not turn into unchecked state expansion

item.title: To avoid backlash, tech giants must share their AI wealth before it’s too late
item.description: The US cannot wait for the worst job losses to hit before moving corporate and public policy in a pro-worker direction

item.title: Tech’s private subsea cables are a threat to everyone else
item.description: By building their own data highways, American hyperscalers are creating a national security risk for other countries
